text: Hello, my name is Dr. Sitch, and today I'll be talking about the rescue stitch for control of hemorrhage in complex robotic surgery. We're going to begin with a case presentation. This case is a patient who is undergoing a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy.

The surgical team dissects along the inferior border of the pancreas using a left-hand bipolar grasper. A small venous tear occurs, and attempts are made to control the bleeding using bipolar energy and a vessel sealer, though initially both methods are ineffective.

The surgeon makes repeated attempts with electrocautery to control hemorrhage from a bleeding vein, but these attempts are unsuccessful. The video shows the importance of adequate exposure and preparation for using a laparoscopic rescue stitch, involving an ethabond laparotai over a knot on a foraproline suture.

Okay, so the next step is... Do you want a needle driver? Yeah, so you've got control, so let's switch out my needle driver. Number three down here. Now the key is not making a whole figure, and that's the only thing this pen roll doesn't do, is make the whole figure. And then you have to get control so he can hand you that needle. Well, I'm going to get control by... Usually we don't have a whole thing we can control, so we're going to have to, like, compress. Maybe I'll try compressing something like this. And then go ahead and knock. And it's all stealing. Hang on, I'll take a peek.

The surgeon exchanges the vessel sealer for a needle driver and loads a 4-0 proline suture with a lapar tie into the abdomen. A simple figure of eight suture is demonstrated to control the bleeding from the arterial or venous injury.

Some important concepts to consider are that the suture does not necessarily have to be the definitive management of your bleeding. Simply throwing your stitch in in a figure of eight fashion or running fashion and then holding up on the needle end of the suture with your left hand will oftentimes give you hemorrhage control and allow you to convert an uncontrollable bleeding episode into a controllable bleeding episode. Options at that point are to hold the needle in the left arm and continue to keep tension on the suture in order to allow you to convert the case to open should you need to. Additionally, you can place another lapper tie on the needle end of the suture and secure the stitch, or you can place another stitch entirely for definitive repair.

During the robotic Whipple procedure, the surgeon encounters bleeding at the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) stump on the specimen side. The dissection continues to develop the portal vein tunnel, and initial attempts to control the bleeding with bipolar electrocautery are shown to exacerbate the bleeding.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to apply pressure to the area of arterial bleeding. The rescue stitch is deployed, and a figure of eight suture is placed to control the bleeding. Both arms are used in tandem to maintain gentle traction on the suture during the second throw, minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon performs an additional third throw of the figure of eight suture to control bleeding, maintaining tension on the suture to prevent further hemorrhage. Difficulty in grasping the needle is addressed by using the pancreatic parenchyma on the specimen side for stabilization. After achieving bleeding control, a laparotide is placed to secure the suture, and the procedure is converted to open due to anticipated challenges in further dissection along the portal vein.

We'd like to emphasize at this point that there are a number of options once you actually have control of bleeding. In this case, we did opt to convert due to anticipated further difficult dissection, but we have successfully used the rescue stitch to avoid conversion in cases where we have had minor venous bleeding up to bleeding involving the vena cava with multiple binotomies and successful repair with the rescue stitch and have avoided converting to open. We hope you found this video useful. As a final recommendation, we do recommend that you practice this in simulation with your robotic team as we feel that rehearsal and preparation are some of the best tools that you can have in your arsenal to prepare for bleeding encountered in complex robotic surgery. Thank you again for your time and thank you for watching.
